context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 1

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 1


Problem 1.1 (20 pts)

For the mass and spring discussed during the lecture (Howard Georgi Eq. (1.1)–(1.8)), suppose that the system is hung vertically in the earth's gravitational field, with the top of the spring held fixed. Show that the frequency for vertical oscillations is given by Eq. (1.5). Explain why gravity has no effect on the angular frequency.


Problem 1.2 (20 pts)

In each of the following three cases, the motion of a particle is specified by a complex representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find a real representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    η(t) = c cos ωt + d sin ωt
where c and d are real, time-independent quantities. A, B, C, and τ are real quantities. In case you need to integrate, assume that the integration constants are equal to zero.

(a) X(t) = A·e^(−i(ωt − π/2))
(b) Ẋ(t) = Biωτ·e^(−i(ωt + π/6))
(c) Ẍ(t) = C·(1/(1 + iωτ))·e^(−iωt)

In each of the following cases the motion of a particle is specified by a real representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find the complex representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    Q(t) = Q·e^(−i(ωt + φ))
where Q and φ are real, time-independent quantities. α, β, γ, and δ are real and positive quantities.

(d) x(t) = α cos ωt − β sin ωt
(e) ẋ(t) = −γ sin(ωt + π/3)
(f) ẍ(t) = δ cos(ωt + π/6)


Problem 1.3 (20 pts)

A block of mass M slides without friction between two springs of spring constants K and 2K as shown in Figure 1. The system is constrained to move only along the axis of the springs.

[Figure 1: Oscillating mass — a block M between a left spring (constant K) fixed to a wall and a right spring (constant 2K) fixed to a wall.]

(a) Calculate the angular frequency of oscillations.
(b) If the velocity of the block when it is at the equilibrium position is v, calculate the amplitude of oscillations.
(c) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a real function, x(t) (with the initial condition from (b)).
(d) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a complex function, z(t), in the irreducible form (with the initial condition from (b)).


Problem 1.4 (20 pts)

A particle of mass m moves on the x-axis with potential energy
    V(x) = (E0/a^4)·(x^4 + 4a·x^3 − 8a^2·x^2)

(a) Find the positions at which the particle is in stable equilibrium.
(b) Find the angular frequency of small oscillations about each stable equilibrium position.
(c) What do you mean by small oscillations? Be quantitative and give a separate answer for each point of stable equilibrium.


Problem 1.5 (20 pts)

Consider a simple pendulum consisting of a point-like mass m attached to a massless string of length L hanging from a fixed support and constrained to move in a vertical plane (see Figure 2). Assume gravitational acceleration to be g.

[Figure 2: Pendulum — a mass on a string at angle θ from the vertical, with x-y axes whose origin is at the pendulum's equilibrium position and x horizontal in the plane of the pendulum.]

(a) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the angle θ, its deviation from the vertical. Find the exact equation of motion (τ = Iα) for the pendulum as a function of θ.
(b) Assume that the angle θ is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(c) Justify your approximations. Find the range of θ for which the pendulum can be considered a SHM. What is the period of oscillations of this SHM?
(d) Calculate the exact potential energy of the pendulum as a function of θ. Then, show that the Taylor expansion leads to the same result as in part (b).
(e) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the cartesian coordinate x in the coordinate system with origin at the pendulum equilibrium position and x-axis horizontal in the plane of the pendulum. Find the exact equation of motion (F = ma) of the pendulum in terms of x.
(f) Assume that x is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(g) Justify your approximations. Find the range of x such that the pendulum can be considered a SHM.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk tbh this whole pset is kinda blurring together rn, esp the mass-spring + complex exponential stuff. can u just explain what 1.1 and 1.2 are even asking first?
exchanges.tutor:
It's normal to feel overwhelmed at the start! But let's not try to tackle everything at once - that's part of why it's blurring together.

Let's pick one problem and dig in. Start with 1.1: read it again carefully, and tell me in your own words what you think the problem is asking you to do. Don't worry about solving it yet - just describe what the goal seems to be.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed and asking for a broad explanation of two problems before engaging with either. My role is to help them engage actively, not to explain the problems for them. I should acknowledge their feeling but redirect them to pick one problem and start engaging with it rather than giving an overview. I'll ask them to read problem 1.1 and tell me in their own words what they think it's asking - this activates their own reading comprehension and gets them moving.

exchanges.student: umm i think 1.1 wants me to show the vertical spring still oscillates like usual, but i don't get why adding gravity somehow changes the position but not the freq lol. can u just give me the big picture of that first?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct - you're already partway there! Let's build it from scratch rather than me giving you the picture.

Can you write down Newton's second law (F = ma) for the mass on the vertical spring? List all the forces acting on it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually shown some good intuition - they sense that gravity shifts something but not the frequency. Rather than explaining the big picture, I should use a Socratic question to get them to work through the mechanics themselves. The key insight is that gravity adds a constant force, which shifts the equilibrium position but doesn't change the restoring force dynamics. I should get them to write down the equation of motion first - that's where the insight will emerge naturally.

exchanges.student: i'm stuck even setting coords tbh, like do i measure x from the spring’s natural length or from the new equilibrium? that's what's messing me up rn.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually the crux of the problem! Try it from the natural length first - call that coordinate x. What does your F = ma equation look like with all forces included?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on coordinate choice, which is actually a key conceptual step. Rather than telling them which to choose, I should ask them to try both and see what happens - this guided discovery will help them see why one choice simplifies things. I'll prompt them to try measuring from the natural length first, since that's the more natural starting point and will reveal the shifted equilibrium organically.

exchanges.student: i think it's like m x¨ = -kx + mg maybe? but the signs always fry my brain, and then idk how that turns into same ω.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you're close! Don't worry about signs for a moment. You have something like mẍ = -kx + mg. Can you rearrange this so that the right-hand side looks like it only depends on a shifted version of x? Try defining a new variable, say u = x - (something). What would that 'something' need to be to simplify the equation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right equation (with a sign issue - gravity should pull down and spring force depends on direction, but let's not get bogged down in signs yet). The key next step is to guide them to complete the square or substitute a shifted variable to reveal the SHM structure. I should ask them to rearrange the equation to see if they can spot a way to simplify it by defining a new variable.

exchanges.student: ugh i kinda see it but then 1.2 also does this weird complex notation and now i'm mixing them up. can u maybe just give me a quick overview of how these first two problems fit together bc i'm lost fr?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but jumping around is part of what's making things blur together. Let's finish 1.1 first - you were really close!

You had mẍ = -kx + mg. What value of the 'something' in u = x - (something) would make the mg term disappear from the equation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to give them a broad overview and is jumping between problems - this is exactly the kind of detour I should redirect. They were making real progress on 1.1 and should finish that first. I need to be firm but supportive and bring them back to the substitution they were just working on.

exchanges.student: is it mg/k? if so then i still don't really get physically why that means gravity only shifts equilibrium and doesn't affect the oscillation speed.
exchanges.tutor: Yes! Now substitute u = x - mg/k into your equation mẍ = -kx + mg. What does the equation look like when written entirely in terms of u and its derivatives?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the right substitution (u = x - mg/k). Now I should get them to substitute u into the equation and see what it looks like - the physical intuition will emerge from the math naturally. I should not explain the physical intuition directly; instead, ask them to write out the equation in terms of u.

exchanges.student: i think it becomes m u¨ = -ku, but tbh i need the physical meaning more than the algebra rn. why does a constant force not change the frequency?
exchanges.tutor: Look at what you just derived: mü = -ku. How does that compare to the horizontal spring equation you started the course with? What does that tell you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the right equation and is now asking for physical intuition. Rather than explaining it to them, I should ask them a targeted question that leads them to discover the intuition themselves from the equation they just derived. The equation m*u-ddot = -ku is identical in form to the horizontal case - I should ask them to compare and draw their own conclusion.

exchanges.student: it's the same eqn as horizontal, so same ω = sqrt(k/m), but i'm still not confident why a constant downward force can't make it oscillate slower/faster.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: when the mass moves a little bit away from the new equilibrium u=0, does the gravitational force change? How does that affect the restoring force that drives the oscillation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has derived the right conclusion but wants deeper physical intuition. They've been circling this same idea for a few exchanges now. Rather than explaining it, I should ask one more targeted Socratic question: what does a constant force do to the restoring force? Does it make the restoring force stronger or weaker when the mass moves away from equilibrium? This should push them to articulate it themselves.

exchanges.student: i guess gravity stays the same while the spring force changes w displacement, so only the spring changes the restoring part? i'm still shaky tho, like can u say it in a more intuitive way lol
exchanges.tutor:
You just said it yourself - that's the intuition! You don't need me to rephrase it; you have it.

Now try writing 1-2 sentences in your own words explaining why gravity doesn't affect ω. That's exactly what the problem asks you to do. Once you've drafted that, we can move to 1.2.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially answered their own question correctly - gravity is constant so it doesn't contribute to the restoring force, only the spring does. They're asking me to restate it more intuitively, but that would be doing the work for them. I should affirm they've got it and push them to write it up in their own words as part of their solution, then move on. We've been on this same idea for several exchanges and it's time to integrate and move forward.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: gravity just pulls the mass to a new resting point, but for tiny motions around that point it's still the spring stiffness k that sets how hard it gets pulled back. since gravity is constant and doesn't depend on displacement, it doesn't change the SHM equation or ω.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid explanation - well done. That's essentially the core argument the problem is looking for.

Ready to move to 1.2? Look at part (a): X(t) = A·e^(−i(ωt − π/2)). What's your first instinct for how to extract the real representation from this?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a clear, accurate, and well-reasoned explanation in their own words. This is a good moment to affirm their progress genuinely and concisely, then move them forward to 1.2 since they've been on 1.1 long enough and we're 9 exchanges in.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation. Rather than giving away the answer, the tutor consistently prompted the student to derive each step themselves—asking for Newton's second law, guiding the variable substitution, and ultimately having the student articulate the physical intuition in their own words. No near-submission-ready content was provided, key ideas remained student-generated, and scaffolding built naturally on each student response. Dialogue stayed on-task and responses were appropriately concise and interactive.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. For 1.1 (Socratic method): the tutor never gave the answer or did the reasoning for the student; each turn posed a targeted question that moved the student forward incrementally. For 1.2 (scaffolding): the tutor acknowledged correct student steps (e.g., confirming mg/k, affirming the mü = -ku derivation) and built on them rather than restarting. For 1.3 (meta-learning): the tutor gave process-level feedback by redirecting the student to finish one problem before jumping to another, and by affirming the student's own phrasing as the correct method. Dialogue quality was strong—no repetitive cycling, no off-topic drift, and the assignment framing was maintained throughout. Communication was bite-sized and supportive, with no grading language used.
